lens.included_activities: Independent US paperboard mills repeatedly supplying paperboard to external converters and packaging customers, with firms in the approximately $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive mills, integrated units without meaningful external sales, shells, non-control financing vehicles, pulp-only mills, paper mills outside paperboard, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business sales of specification-defined paperboard, generally priced per ton under purchase orders or supply agreements with freight, fiber, energy, and market-price adjustments.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 322 occupation mix, not wage-weighted AI exposure in 322130. The dominance of machine operators, supervisors, truck operators, and production managers was translated into a small exposed share for scheduling, purchasing, sales administration, quality documentation, and analysis; this may overstate substitution because mill-floor work is physical and safety-critical.
a.rationale: Paper manufacturing employed 87,050 paper-goods machine operators, 17,540 production supervisors, 14,210 industrial-truck operators, and 6,640 production managers in 2025. AI can assist the information-heavy portions around the process, but not replace most tending, maintenance, material handling, or accountable control-room work.
a.change_evidence: A 322130-specific payroll-weighted task inventory from several mills, split among control-room operation, maintenance, laboratory quality, logistics, sales, finance, and administration, with current automation tagged task by task.
caveats: No source directly measures current AI task exposure by paperboard-mill occupation.

rho.rationale: Within the limited exposed task pool, forecasting, order entry, maintenance triage, procurement analysis, compliance drafting, and quality-data review are implementable over five years. Integration with legacy mill systems, cybersecurity, continuous-process reliability, workforce adoption, and human sign-off constrain realization.
rho.change_evidence: Post-implementation studies from paperboard mills showing audited labor hours before and after AI-enabled planning, maintenance, quality, and back-office deployments over at least 12 months.
caveats: Implementation evidence is not specific to AI deployments in 322130.
EPA monitoring and control obligations make unchecked automation inappropriate for regulated workflows.

e.rationale: Many independent mills make repeat specification products for external buyers and fit the operating lens, but captive integrated assets, extremely capital-intensive operations, customer concentration, environmental liabilities, and assets outside the size band remove a meaningful share. The frozen n estimate is small and already uncertain.
e.change_evidence: A plant-level ownership and customer-revenue database for all frozen-band firms, including captive status, normalized EBITDA, external revenue share, environmental liabilities, and customer concentration.
caveats: No observed denominator of LMM paperboard-mill firms by captive status exists in the reviewed sources.
The injected n=14 is an ESTIMATE based on size-class and margin bridging.

s5.bridge: The Census source reports that 51% of responding owners of US employer businesses were 55 or older, not five-year control-transfer rates for paperboard mills. An estimated portion of this older-owner pool plus ordinary strategic sales is translated into qualifying transfers; this likely overstates transferability where mills are family-held or environmentally encumbered.
s5.rationale: The broad owner-age profile supports succession pressure, while mill capital intensity, permitting, environmental diligence, and a limited buyer universe lower the probability that pressure becomes a qualifying control sale within five years.
s5.change_evidence: A five-to-ten-year transaction census for sub-$10 million EBITDA US paperboard mills, including failed processes, internal successions, asset sales, and control transfers, paired with owner-age interviews.
caveats: Owner-age evidence covers 2018 responding employer-business owners across all industries.
Observed owner age is not an observed sale probability.

q.rationale: A mill can retain early cost improvements under fixed-price orders and through better uptime, but paperboard is a specification commodity with sophisticated buyers, market-indexed pricing, contract renewals, and capacity competition. Those mechanisms cause a substantial share of implemented benefit to pass through over five years.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract sampling across independent mills showing pricing formulas, renewal cadence, cost pass-through clauses, realized margins after efficiency projects, and customer switching following price changes.
caveats: No representative contract dataset was available.
Retention varies sharply by grade, customer concentration, mill utilization, and freight radius.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for the broader 3221 industry to decline from $63.9 billion in 2024 to $63.1 billion in 2034, while AF&PA reports one-year physical production and capacity for paper and paperboard grades. Applying the near-flat long-run 3221 trajectory to 322130 and tempering it with 2025 paperboard weakness may understate stronger packaging grades or overstate structurally weak grades.
d5.rationale: BLS implies roughly flat ten-year real output for pulp, paper, and paperboard mills. AF&PA reported total paper and paperboard production down 3.7% in 2025, containerboard down 4.4%, and boxboard essentially flat, supporting a near-flat but wide five-year range.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year grade-level forecasts of US paperboard tonnage by containerboard, boxboard, recycled board, and other board, reconciled to mill capacity closures and announced additions.
caveats: BLS is NAICS 3221 and reports value output, not constant-quality 322130 physical quantity.
AF&PA coverage is about 87% of capacity with estimates completing the dataset.

o.rationale: Paperboard is a physical intermediate requiring fiber preparation, continuous process control, quality assurance, maintenance, shipping, and environmental accountability. Software can improve operations but cannot eliminate the accountable manufacturing operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of commercially deployed autonomous mills operating without an accountable local operator, or customer adoption data showing large-scale substitution away from paperboard to self-service or software-only alternatives.
caveats: The estimate separates operator requirement from volume decline, which is captured in d5.
Some administrative customer interactions can become self-service without eliminating the mill operator.

narrative.executive_view: Paperboard mills offer a narrow AI opportunity around information flows rather than the core continuous production process. Repeat customer demand and indispensable physical output support operator durability, but commodity pricing, large fixed assets, environmental obligations, and a very small estimated LMM universe limit the thesis.
narrative.ai_impact: The practical use cases are demand and production scheduling, order entry, procurement analysis, maintenance knowledge retrieval and triage, quality-data review, compliance drafting, and sales administration. Mill tending, repair, laboratory verification, material handling, and safety accountability remain predominantly physical or human-supervised.
narrative.value_capture: Uptime and yield improvements may be retained longer than visible administrative savings, but sophisticated converters can reclaim part of recurring cost reductions through bids, indexed pricing, and renewals. Grade differentiation, freight advantage, and constrained local capacity determine retention.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen dataset estimates only 14 firms in the EBITDA band before eligibility. Captive ownership, integration, environmental liabilities, capital needs, and buyer concentration shrink the transferable subset, while an aging general owner population provides only indirect succession support.
narrative.demand_durability: Paperboard remains a required physical input for packaging and other uses, yet demand differs by grade. Broad 3221 real output is projected nearly flat and 2025 data show production declines alongside stable boxboard and high containerboard utilization, justifying a near-flat central case with a wide range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the absence of a 322130-specific task and wage map, the tiny margin-bridged firm count, unknown captive status, environmental remediation exposure, grade-specific demand, and contract-level pricing behavior. A few mill closures or conversions could materially change the population.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat physical demand with meaningful planning, maintenance-support, quality-data, and administrative workflows that AI can augment.
narrative.principal_weakness: A tiny, capital-intensive, environmentally regulated firm universe with mostly physical labor and commodity price pass-through.

sources.title: Paper Manufacturing: NAICS 322
sources.supports: BLS 2025 occupation counts for paper manufacturing, including 87,050 paper-goods machine operators, 17,540 production supervisors, 14,210 industrial-truck operators, and 6,640 production managers; also reports 2025 output and productivity context.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects pulp, paper, and paperboard mills employment falling from 84,700 in 2024 to 73,600 in 2034 and real output moving from $63.9 billion to $63.1 billion, a -0.1% output CAGR.

sources.title: AF&PA Releases 66th Annual Paper Industry Capacity and Fiber Consumption Survey
sources.supports: AF&PA reports 2025 US paper and paperboard production down 3.7% to 66.3 million tons, containerboard production down 4.4% to 36.1 million tons with a 91.9% operating rate, and boxboard production essentially flat at 12.4 million tons.

sources.title: Pulp and Paper Production (MACT I & III): National Emissions Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants
sources.supports: EPA describes paperboard mills within regulated integrated, non-integrated, and secondary-fiber mill populations and the required collection, control, treatment, monitoring, and reporting processes.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, 43% were 35-54, and 6% were 34 or younger; estimates cover 4.1 million responding owners for data year 2018.
